title: Clojure Macros Cheat Sheet
description: Essential Clojure macros for metaprogramming and code transformation.

commands.description: Defines a macro 'unless' that acts as an inverse of 'if'.
commands.scenario: Use when you need to execute code only if a condition is false.
Use when you want to bind a value and execute code only if the value is truthy.

commands.description: Defines a macro 'or' that evaluates expressions until one is truthy.
commands.scenario: Use when you need to return the first truthy value from a list of expressions.

commands.description: Defines a macro 'and' that evaluates expressions until one is falsey.
commands.scenario: Use when you need to ensure all expressions are truthy.

commands.command: (defmacro cond [& clauses] (when clauses `(if ~(first clauses) ~(second clauses) (cond ~@(nnext clauses)))))
commands.description: Defines a macro 'cond' for multiple conditional branches.
commands.scenario: Use when you need to evaluate multiple conditions in sequence.

commands.command: (defmacro -> [x & forms] (reduce (fn [x f] (if (seq? f) (with-meta `(~(first f) ~x ~@(next f)) (meta f)) `(~f ~x))) x forms))
commands.description: Defines a threading macro '->' for left-to-right function application.
commands.scenario: Use when you want to apply a series of transformations to a value.

commands.command: (defmacro ->> [x & forms] (reduce (fn [x f] (if (seq? f) (with-meta `(~(first f) ~@(next f) ~x) (meta f)) `(~f ~x))) x forms))
commands.description: Defines a threading macro '->>' for right-to-left function application.
commands.scenario: Use when you want to apply a series of transformations to a value, passing it as the last argument.
Use when you need to cache the results of expensive function calls.

commands.command: (defmacro time [expr] `(let [start# (. System (nanoTime)) result# ~expr] (println "Elapsed time:" (/ (double (- (. System (nanoTime)) start#)) 1000000.0) "msecs") result#))
commands.description: Measures the execution time of an expression.
commands.scenario: Use when you need to benchmark the performance of a code block.

commands.command: (defmacro doseq [[item coll] & body] `(loop [coll# ~coll] (when (seq coll#) (let [~item (first coll#)] ~@body) (recur (rest coll#)))))
commands.description: Iterates over a collection, executing body for each item.
commands.scenario: Use when you need to perform side effects for each item in a collection.
Use when you need to transform a collection into another collection.
Use when you need to define a sequence that is computed on demand.
